slug: 普罗米修斯偷了火被罚每天被啄肝脏_但他没后悔
title: 普罗米修斯偷了火被罚每天被啄肝脏——但他没后悔

outline.concept: Regret requires surprise — and Prometheus had none. As a Titan with prophecy, he saw the eagle, the liver, the centuries of daily mutilation before he ever touched the fire. Regret typically hits when outcomes violate expectations; when you chose with full foreknowledge, the cost was already baked into the decision.
outline.query: 明知后果还是做了 预料之中 不意外 knew what would happen still chose

outline.concept: The liver was chosen because it regenerates — the punishment targets will, not flesh. A mortal wound ends suffering; regeneration ensures the torture never ends and never numbs. Zeus didn't want Prometheus dead — he wanted him to recant. The daily renewal makes the question of regret the actual battlefield.
outline.query: 反复承受 不断重来 没麻木 撑住 willpower daily repeated pain

outline.concept: Irreversible acts create structural immunity to regret. Fire, once given to humans, cannot be recalled — it spreads by nature. Regret requires the fantasy that things could be otherwise. When your act is permanent and uncontainable, regret becomes cognitively useless: you can't un-give the fire, so the only coherent responses are to affirm the choice or suffer twice.
outline.query: 无法挽回 回不去了 做了就做了 irreversible no going back permanent

outline.concept: Chosen suffering consolidates identity rather than destroying it. When you endure for a choice you'd make again, the suffering becomes proof of conviction, not evidence of error. Prometheus on the rock isn't Zeus's victim — he's the author of his own sentence. To regret would be to unmake himself, which is a worse destruction than any eagle.
outline.query: 认了 甘愿 这就是我 自己选的 chosen suffering identity 甘愿受苦
